Cursor AI IDE rewrites the developer workflow — not just autocomplete, but a full AI agent that edits multiple files from one sentence. Here's what it actually does, how it compares to GitHub Copilot, and whether the switch is worth it.

One prompt. Four files changed. No line written by hand. That is what Cursor's Agent Mode looks like in practice — and it's why over a million developers switched in 2024 alone.

🎯 **What you'll learn**
- What makes Cursor an AI-native IDE (not just a VS Code plugin)
- How Cursor's Tab, Chat, and Agent Mode work — and when to use each
- How Cursor compares to GitHub Copilot, Windsurf, and Claude Code
- What "vibecoding" means and why top developers are doing it
- The real risks of AI-generated code and how to review it properly
- How to get started with Cursor's free tier today

🔗 **Links**
- Cursor: https://cursor.com/
- Cursor Docs: https://docs.cursor.com/
- GitHub Copilot: https://github.com/features/copilot
- Cursor community: https://www.reddit.com/r/cursor/

Cursor AI IDE represents a genuine step-change in how software gets built — not because it replaces developers, but because it changes what developers spend their time doing. If you're comparing cursor vs github copilot, evaluating an ai code editor in 2025, or just curious what an ai native ide development workflow actually feels like, this breakdown covers the full picture. The developer skill that matters most now isn't typing speed — it's knowing how to direct an AI and review what it builds.
